coredata.dc:title: A case study to predict the capacity fade of the battery of electrified vehicles in real-world use conditions

coredata.dc:description: This work aims at combining recent capacity fade performance-based models for Lithium-ion batteries with the real-world vehicle driving data to develop a case study for predicting the capacity fade of the battery of electric vehicles. The study adopts the calendar and cycle capacity fade of three Li-ion chemistries in three different battery architectures combined with five different recharging strategies, delivering 220 scenarios in total. The results show that most of the combinations of Li-ion chemistries, battery architectures, and recharge strategies do not lead to battery capacity drop below 80% of its nominal value in less than 5 calendar years for a driving profile of up to 1000 km/month. At higher monthly mileage, LiFePO4 and NCM battery chemistries with spinel Mn might have values below 5 years. Instead, NCM-LMO appears not to go below this threshold regardless of the mileage for a 16S-72P-6S battery architecture, with the first life above 10 years. The work also includes an experimental validation with a comparison between capacity fade predictions of NCM-LMO model with real-world measurements from two test-vehicles in a mileage accumulation test campaign.

row.entry: Wang et al. (2014) and Purewal et al. (2014)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Assumptions for implementing calendar and cycle capacity fade models in TEMA.

row.entry: Capacity fade is calculated at the cell level, assuming that all cells exhibit the same fade value, and that this is also the fade of the battery itself (uniform ageing assumption). The model assumes average quantities in the reference period per each vehicle considered for DoD, C-rate, Ah-throughput and temperature, thus implying constant charge-discharge SoC swing per cell. Although real-world non-constant cycles are not accounted in this work, they do not significantly affect capacity fade, as shown in Sarasketa-Zabala et al. (2016). Moreover, Paterson et Al. recognise the integral value of the energy processed by the cell to compute the total capacity fade for vehicle applications (Paterson et al., 2010), thus further strengthening this assumption.

Cell level DoD and temperature are assumed to be equal to values for the entire battery, consistent with the uniform fade assumption. C-rate and Ah-throughput are scaled down from the battery level to the cell level by considering the battery pack electric architecture; see Table 3.

Battery temperature is assumed to be regulated by the battery management system (BMS) to between 22 °C and 27 °C during the driving and recharging phases (cycle capacity fade modelling), and equal to the ambient temperature during the parking phase (calendar capacity fade modelling). Previous studies have shown that fade is minimised when the battery is kept at ambient temperature (i.e. approximately 20 °C) in operation and storage (Bandhauer et al., 2011) and that BMS typically need to cool down the battery pack to minimise capacity fade (Neubauer and Wood, 2014).

The total capacity fade is calculated as net of the capacity fade reserve. This is a region of the SoC that is used to balance the loss of capacity of the cell during the first life. The model assumes that the battery’s usable SoC window goes from 20% to 95% at the beginning of life (BoL) and from 5% to 95% at the EoL for BEVs, thus imposing a capacity reserve fade of 15% SoC. This value is considered representative for BEVs, while it is typically 25–30% SoC for PHEVs. Total capacity fade of the vehicle is therefore calculated as per eq. (17), which considers calendar ageing and cycle ageing as additive components of total ageing, according to Wang et al. (2016), Smith et al. (2012) and Marano et al. (2009).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note that Strategy 2 does not apply to PHEV-1, because high power DC recharge is generally not applicable to PHEVs. Thus, the in-vehicle battery durability scenarios presented in this work include 3 reference vehicles (1 PHEV and 2 BEVs), combined with 4 recharge strategies for the PHEV and 5 recharge strategies for the BEVs, and 4 combinations of calendar and cycle capacity fade models, allowing to 56 different scenarios.

head.ce:title: A case study to predict the capacity fade of the battery of electrified vehicles in real-world use conditions

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work aims at combining recent capacity fade performance-based models for Lithium-ion batteries with the real-world vehicle driving data to develop a case study for predicting the capacity fade of the battery of electric vehicles. The study adopts the calendar and cycle capacity fade of three Li-ion chemistries in three different battery architectures combined with five different recharging strategies, delivering 220 scenarios in total. The results show that most of the combinations of Li-ion chemistries, battery architectures, and recharge strategies do not lead to battery capacity drop below 80% of its nominal value in less than 5 calendar years for a driving profile of up to 1000 km/month. At higher monthly mileage, LiFePO4 and NCM battery chemistries with spinel Mn might have values below 5 years. Instead, NCM-LMO appears not to go below this threshold regardless of the mileage for a 16S-72P-6S battery architecture, with the first life above 10 years. The work also includes an experimental validation with a comparison between capacity fade predictions of NCM-LMO model with real-world measurements from two test-vehicles in a mileage accumulation test campaign.

ce:def-description.ce:para: electric vehicles and the environment informal working group
working party on pollution and energy
UN’s world forum for harmonization of vehicle regulations

ce:section.ce:para: Though electric vehicles (EVs) currently represent only a small share of automotive sales in most of the world’s markets, they are expected to achieve larger shares as battery costs decrease, becoming competitive against conventionally fuelled vehicles. Forecasts estimate that EVs will account for more than two-thirds of the sales of new passenger cars by 2050 (International Energy Agency, 2011; Ertrac – Eposs – Smartgrids, 2012), with presence in all major regions of the world. To satisfy this increasing demand, automotive industry is pushing the boundaries of Li-ion technology, today adopted on the majority of plug-in hybrid electric vehicles (PHEV) and in all battery electric vehicles (BEV) configurations (Miller, 2015). This has resulted in numerous variants of Li-ion batteries, that can be grouped according to the chemistry of the cathode: lithium-cobalt-oxide (LCO), lithium-iron-phosphate (LiFePO4), nickel-cobalt-manganese (NCM) and lithium-manganese-oxide (LMO). These differ for the base materials employed, for the associated production cost and for the performance delivered (i.e. energy density), being positioned at different steps of the European reference development roadmap for batteries (Steen et al., 2017).

A major factor for evaluating the cost and the environmental impact of traction batteries is their durability; this is defined as the combination of time and/or mileage needed to reach a certain percentage drop of the battery capacity, starting from its nominal capacity value (i.e. new battery condition). At today a unique definition of durability in the electric vehicle market does not yet exists, although public attention for this aspect is increasing, being this a major cost factor to be evaluated for a potential EV buyer. So far OEMs established their own durability requirements and testing procedure, covering the battery durability (Nissan Automotive, 2018). Although this approach was essential for the successful deployment of the earliest fleets of electric vehicles, it might not be enough in future, calling for a better regulation for in-vehicle battery durability requirements. Beyond the vehicle warranty, there is the environmental aspect to consider too. Batteries of EVs need to be durable for allowing electric vehicle to reach the life-cycle emissions break-even point against gasoline and diesel vehicles and, this consideration, must also account for different impacts for BEVs and PHEVs. In a BEV, battery degradation can reduce the overall utility of the vehicle, though it does not significantly affect upstream emissions associated with charging the vehicle. In a PHEV, it is unlikely to reduce the overall utility of the vehicle, though it can result in increased operation of the combustion engine and higher tailpipe emissions. Hence, understanding the battery degradation mechanisms and quantifying battery lifetime in real-world use conditions is key for evaluating the economic, social and environmental impact of electric mobility. This paper aims at addressing this need by combining state-of-the-art battery calendar and cycle capacity fade models with big data studies on real-world conventional vehicle driving patterns to develop a scenario-based analysis of the in-vehicle performance degradation of Li-ion automotive batteries. Its innovativeness consists in the fact that, for the first time, big data from transport has been used to estimate the degradation of batteries of EVs, accounting for different electrical architectures, Li-ion chemistries, recharge strategies and usage patterns and identifying which elements are most likely to determine the End of Life (EoL) of the battery. The results provide key indications to formulate the policy directions to regulate, if any necessity arises, in-vehicle battery durability and the study is, currently, unique on its own.

This work has been developed to inform the Electric Vehicles and the Environment Informal Working Group (EVE-IWG). This group is a subsidiary of the UN’s World Forum for Harmonization of Vehicle Regulations (WP.29), and part of its current mandate includes exploring the topic of battery performance and durability and its implications on the environmental performances of EVs (United Nations, Economic and Social Council, 2016). The results presented in this paper constitute part of the scientific efforts undertaken within this working group and are expected to be reported to the EVE-IWG’s parent body, i.e. the Working Party on Pollution and Energy (GRPE). The manuscript is structured as follows:
•
Section 2.1 presents a quick overview of the electrochemical ageing mechanisms that can affect lithium cells at the anode and cathode levels, relating them to capacity and power fade;
•
Section 2.2 and the sub Sections 2.2.1–2.2.4 present a literature review of the ageing models adopted as reference in this work, which are all derived from literature. Where the model’s coefficients were not published, the authors recalculated them based on the original experimental data available in literature;
•
Sections 2.3–2.5 illustrate how the models presented in the sections above have been implemented in the Joint Research Centre (JRC) platform TEMA (Transport tEchnology and Mobility Assessment).
•
Section 3 presents the results of the mobility data (3.1), capacity fade models (3.2) and estimation of the EoL under different user scenarios (3.3). Section 3.4 presents vehicle-level validation of the modelling results;
•
Sections 3.5 and 4. highlight the limitations of the presented results and authors’ concluding remarks.

Mechanisms (1), (2), (4) and (5) are responsible for cell capacity fade, i.e. reduction of its energy capacity, while mechanism (3) is responsible for cell power fade, i.e. reduction of its output power capability. Mechanism (6) generates a combination of both capacity and power fade by accelerating the other mechanisms at anode. Three macroscopic effects can be recognised as result of cell ageing:
•
Primary loss of cyclable Li, where diminished Li availability results in capacity fade (Abraham et al., 2005);
•
Secondary loss of electrode active materials, where anode/cathode surface decreases result in the capacity fade (Zhang and White, 2008);
•
Increase of internal resistance within the cell, resulting in power fade (Murphy et al., 2001).

As depicted in Section 2.1, electrochemical ageing of battery cells is a complex phenomenon, determined by a combination of mechanisms that impact the electrodes and electrolyte. Despite such complexity, the visible effects are simply a loss of energy capacity and a decrease of output power of the cell. Hence, an ageing model must aim at reproducing these two effects while considering the input variables identified above. The ageing estimation models can be classified in five main categories (Barré et al., 2013): electrochemical models, equivalent circuit-based models, performance-based models, analytical models and statistical models. They differ in the conceptual approach, resulting in different fields of applicability; for the scopes of this work the performance-based models have been adopted, given their relative low computational burden, compatible for being coupled with large-scale real-world driving data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LiFePO4 capacity fade model (Wang et al., 2011)
ce:section.ce:para:
This model estimates the capacity fade due to cycle ageing of LiFePO4 cells. It is based on work published by Wang et al. (2011), where cell life measurements were collected based on a test matrix that included three parameters: temperature (from −30 to 60 °C), DoD (from 90% to 10%), and discharge rate (i.e. C-rate, from C/2 to 10C, with the 1C rate corresponding to 2A). The model predicts the percentage of capacity loss (Qloss) expressed as a combination of the Arrhenius law to account for the temperature effect with a power law to account for the battery use, generalised in Eq. (1) below:

The main limitation of this model is that it is not applicable at low temperatures, i.e. below 0 °C, and is recommended for applications between 15 and 60 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LiFePO4 capacity and power fade models (Sarasketa-Zabala et al., 2013–2015)
ce:section.ce:para:
This model estimates the capacity fade due to calendar and cycle ageing and the power fade due to cycle ageing of LiFePO4 cells. It is based the works published between 2013 and 2015 by Sarasketa-Zabala et al. (2013–2015), and it has been applied by Sarasketa-Zabala et al. to estimate the lifetime of electric vehicles combined with vehicle-to-grid (V2G) energy transfer in 2016 (Sarasketa-Zabala et al., 2016). The coefficients of Eqs. (5) and (7) are known, while those from Eq. (6) are not. Therefore, Eq. (6) is fitted to experimental data of Fig. 1 by using the fit function in Matlab (R2015b) (Mathworks Inc., 2017). The non-linear least squares problem was solved using a damped least-square algorithm, i.e. the Levenberg–Marquardt (LM), in Least Absolute Residuals (LAR) formulation. This method finds a curve that minimizes the absolute difference between residuals and is less influenced by outliers than other algorithms based on squared differences. Fig. 2 depicts the interpolant surfaces calculated for 10% ≤ DoD ≤ 50% in (a) and DoD < 10% or DoD > 50% in (b) and their respective residuals. These are satisfactorily small for all considered cases, except for the tails of the curves at 5% and 100% DoD above 10,000 Ah-throughput, in accordance with (Sarasketa-Zabala et al., 2015). Eq. (8) reports the calculated coefficients and the R-square values for the interpolant surfaces of Fig. 2, while Fig. 3 depicts a comparison between the model predictions and the experimental values from Fig. 1, for all the DoD and C-rates available.
(8)

The agreement shown in Fig. 3 highlights that Eq. (6) fit well the experimental data at 1C, 2C and 3.5C, despite not being dependant on C-rate. Moreover, Eq. (6) also includes a parameter k2 to account for non-constant DoD and C-rates over time, being the first more prominent than the second and setting a value below one to account for this (Sarasketa-Zabala et al., 2015). For simplicity, k2 is set equal to 1 in this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NCM plus spinel Mn capacity fade model (Wang et al., 2014)
ce:section.ce:para:
This model estimates the capacity fade due to calendar and cycle ageing for NCM plus spinel Mn cells. It is based on the works of Wang et al. (2014) and Purewal et al. (2014) where cell life measurements were collected based on a test matrix that included the same parameters as per the model presented in Section 2.2.1: temperatures (from 10 to 46 °C), DoD (from 90% to 10%), and discharge rates (i.e. C-rate, from C/2 to 6.5C). The model consists of equations for calendar capacity fade (9) and cycle capacity fade (10), whose parameters are summarised in (11).

Similarly, T is the cell temperature in K, while t is calendar time in days and Ah is the total ampere-hours exchanged by the battery. It is interesting to notice the similarity between Eqs. (9) and (3) for the calendar capacity fade. Both equations assume that calendar capacity fade is dependent on the square root of the time and the Arrhenius law for the temperature. The dependency on the SoC is weak, as per the 0.01 coefficient in (3). A similar equation form is assumed for the cycle capacity fade in (1), but equation (10) assumes the Arrhenius exponential factor combined with a polynomial form for temperature. Note also that Eq. (10) is very sensitive to the decimal place of the coefficients of the polynomial, thus these coefficients need to be accurate to the fourth decimal place.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NCM – LMO capacity and power fade models (Cordoba-Arenas et al., 2015)
ce:section.ce:para:
This model estimates the capacity and power fade due to cycle ageing for NCM-LMO cells. It is based on the work of Cordoba-Arenas et al. (2015). This work investigates capacity and power fade due to cycling of pouch cells for PHEVs and considers the fraction of time the vehicle spends in Charge Depleting (CD) and Charge Sustaining (CS) modes. This concept is introduced through a ratio defined according to (12), which is equal to one for BEVs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Implementation in TEMA and scale-up of the cell-based capacity fade models to the vehicle-level
ce:section.ce:para: Table 1 reports a summary of the models, classified according to their reference battery cathode chemistries.

Four different combinations of these models have been adopted to calculate the capacity fade of in-vehicle batteries:
•
#1 (LiFePO4): Sarasketa-Zabala et al. (2013, 2014) model for calendar ageing plus Wang et al. (2011) model for cycle ageing (i.e. Eqs. (3) and (1);
•
#2 (LiFePO4): Sarasketa-Zabala et al. (2013, 2014) model for calendar ageing plus Sarasketa-Zabala et al. (2015) model for cycle ageing with the coefficients calculated in Section 2.2.2 (i.e. Eqs. (3) and (6), with coefficients in Eq. (8),
•
#3 (NCM + Spinel Mn): Wang et al. (2014) for calendar ageing plus Wang et al. (2014)) for cycle (i.e. Eqs. (9) and (10);
•
#4 (NCM-LMO): Wang et al. (2014) for calendar ageing plus Cordoba-Arenas et al. (2015) for cycle ageing (i.e. Eqs. (9) and (13)).

The four selected combinations have been implemented in the EU Commission platform TEMA, (De Gennaro et al., 2016a). It is a modular big data platform designed to reproduce large-scale mobility behaviours from GPS data of vehicles, with an actual available database that includes 691,751 vehicles, equivalent to 146.7 million km reconstructed with 2.75 billion records across 19 EU member states (Paffumi et al., 2018a). TEMA is used to quantify possible impacts of new vehicle technologies on real-world mobility and develop scenarios to assess the impact of policy actions in transport. Examples of its previous applications include large scale mobility analyses (De Gennaro et al., 2014a; Paffumi et al., 2018a,b) electric mobility assessment (Paffumi et al., 2015), its impact on the grid (De Gennaro et al., 2014b; Chaouachi et al., 2016) and V2G (Paffumi et al., 2016), deployment of alternative fuel infrastructure (De Gennaro et al., 2015a), quantification of real-world non-driving emissions (Martini et al., 2014; De Gennaro et al., 2016b), evaluation of the Utility Factor (UF) for electric fleets (Paffumi et al., 2018c) and support of the eco-innovation technologies assessments (Lodi et al., 2018). In this work TEMA has been extended by implementing the calendar and cycle capacity fade models and combining them with driving data to simulate EV deployment scenarios with different driving styles, recharge patterns, battery architectures and environmental conditions. The capacity fade models adopted in this work describe cell-level operation. They need to be scaled-up at the vehicle-level to be combined with TEMA. This is done using the assumptions listed in Table 2 . Note that this work has been performed focusing on one subset of data from the Italian province of Modena. It does include 16.9 million records (representative of 14.98 million km and 2.64 million trips) acquired in a period of one calendar month (i.e. May 2011).

To assess the battery durability performance, the authors considered three reference battery packs associated with three reference vehicles available on the market before the year 2018. These three vehicles have different battery shapes and electric architectures and are used as guidelines for the present study. Therefore, the presented results should not be taken as definitive predictions for their durability, neither must be intended to specifically address these vehicles, because of the differences between their battery chemistries and those considered in Sections 2.2.1–2.2.4. The vehicles are generically labelled as PHEV-1, BEV-1 and BEV-2. PHEV-1 adopts a T-shaped battery pack with the top of the T under the rear seat and has a similar architecture to a Chevrolet Volt. It is made of 192 pouch cells connected in 7 modules: 4 modules of 24 cells each and 3 modules of 32 cells each. These constitute two parallel legs of 96 cells each, connected in series, thus resulting in a 2P-96S architecture. BEV-1 adopts instead a parallelepiped battery, hidden under the rear seats and floor and has a similar architecture to a Nissan LEAF. It is made of 192 pouch cells too, arranged in 48 modules connected in series, each made of 4 cells arranged in 2 parallel legs of 2 cells each, thus resulting in a 48S-2P-2S architecture. BEV-2 has a flat battery pack (sometimes referred as “skateboard”) under the floor of the vehicle, made of 6912 cylindrical cells arranged in 16 modules each made of 432 cells; each module is configured as 72 parallel legs, each containing 6 cells in series, thus resulting in 16S-72P-6S architecture. It has a similar architecture to a Tesla Model-S. PHEV-1 and BEV-1 have a simpler battery architecture than BEV-2, given by the different cell type adopted (pouch versus cylindrical), resulting in lower manufacturing cost. On the other hand, the significantly larger number of cells adopted for BEV-2 and the more complex architecture allows for a significantly lower C-rate and Ah-throughput at a cell level, with a beneficial effect on cycle capacity fade, as shown in section 3.

Table 3 reports the main features of these three reference battery packs. Table 4 reports the assumed usable energy criteria for the reference vehicles. Each battery is assumed to have a BoL usable energy equal to 75% of the nominal capacity reported in Table 3, i.e. 12 kWh for the PHEV-1, 18 kWh for BEV-1 and 63.75 kWh for BEV-2. Moreover, each battery is considered to reach its EoL when the usable energy becomes equal to 80% of its respective BoL value. In addition, each battery allows for an energy reserve value, equal to 25% of the nominal capacity for PHEV-1 and 15% of the nominal capacity for BEV-1 and BEV-2. This means that PHEV-1 uses as reserve the full capacity that is left out at BoL, while BEV-1 and BEV-2 only use 15% out of the 25% of the full capacity left out at BoL. In other words, the BEVs’ batteries, regardless of the ageing, are always used in a capacity range from 5% to 95% SoC, to preserve their capacity. Table 4 also reports the assumed distance specific energy consumption for the reference vehicles, as declared by the manufacturer or from real drive-tests performed by the authors (US EPA, 2012; De Gennaro et al., 2015b,c). These values are considered conservative and representative of real-world use of the vehicles including an average use of the auxiliary systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recharge strategies and in-vehicle battery durability scenarios
ce:section.ce:para:
As described in De Gennaro et al. (2016c), TEMA replicates the driving behaviour from the selected driving datasets presented in Section 2.3 by using a vehicle fleet consisting of the reference vehicles in Table 4. The driving is coupled with recharge behavioural models, which aim at representing the most likely recharging behaviours, depending on the individual choices of the driver and on the recharge infrastructure available. They are based on data reported in the literature (Parness, 2011) and include:
•
probability based non-opportunistic/opportunistic recharge (i.e. simulating a vehicle that is only recharged during 40% of parking events that meet a certain time threshold);
•
on-peak/off-peak recharge (i.e. the vehicle is recharged when grid load is at a minimum);
•
time/use-constrained recharge (i.e. the vehicle is recharged when the time-window and/or the price constraints set by the owner are met, or when the grid decides to supply the energy according to its electric load).
Among the fifteen strategies considered in Paffumi et al. (2015), only the four reported in Table 5 are presented in this paper, consistently with De Gennaro et al. (2014b, 2015a).

Strategy 1 (Long-Stop Random AC): the recharge requires a vehicle stop longer than 120 min (long-stop). It uses an AC single-phase power line at 3.3 kW (IEC 62,196 Mode 1/2 (Paffumi et al., 2015). It represents the home/office-recharge. The effective power is scaled down to a constant value of 2 kW to account for the recharging profile (i.e. power modulation applied from the vehicle), and it is subject to a random-generated threshold parameter, which makes it happen only with a probability of 40%. This aims at representing the possible unavailability of a recharging station or a human choice/mistake, i.e. the driver forgets or chooses not to recharge.

Strategy 2 (Short-Stop Random DC): the recharge requires a vehicle stop longer than 20 min, (i.e. short-stop) and uses a high-power DC infrastructure (55 kW, IEC 62,196 Mode 4, (Paffumi et al., 2015). The random threshold is the same as per Strategy 1 and it is representative of the recharge that could take place in parking lots equipped with fast-charging devices. In this case, the recharge power is scaled down to a constant value of 40 kW, to account for the recharging profile.

Strategy 3 (Night AC) and Strategy 4 (Smart AC): the vehicle recharges when it is parked in a specific time window between 22.00 and 07.00 for the Night AC and in a 4-hour (±2 h) window around the minimum load on the electricity grid (i.e. between 4.00 and 7.00 in the morning, depending on the day of the week, (ENSTO-E, 2018) for the Smart AC. No random threshold is applied. For the Night AC (Strategy 3) the parking event must be longer than 4 h. The recharge is applied in AC (3.3 kW, IEC 62,196 Mode 1/2 (Paffumi et al., 2015) and the power is scaled down to 2 kW as in Strategy 1. The Night AC (Strategy 3) represents the typical overnight home recharge, while the Smart AC (Strategy 4) represents the recharge that might be associated with a discounted electricity price for the consumer.

Strategy 5 (Long-Stop AC, (3-phases)): the recharge requires a vehicle stop longer than 120 min and is with a 3-phases power line (i.e. AC, 3-phases at 10 kW, IEC 62,196 Mode 1/2 (Paffumi et al., 2015). It represents a recharge happening in a dedicated infrastructure (i.e. offices, shopping malls, airports or train station parking lots) like the Level 2 SAE charging infrastructure common in North American (ACL2). The recharge power is scaled down to a constant value of 9 kW to account for the recharging it is not subject to a threshold parameter.

The charging efficiency has been set equal to 95% (Paffumi et al., 2015) for all strategies, according to values measured by the authors in De Gennaro et al. (2015b). The effects of ambient temperature conditions, seasonal variation of driver habits and electric energy consumption have all been disregarded in the analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Urban mobility: potential of electric vehicles in replacing conventional fuel vehicles
ce:section.ce:para: Table 6 shows a summary of the results for the database of the province of Modena, for each reference vehicle and recharge strategy. The first column of this table reports the “full electric fleet”; this is calculated as the number of vehicles, and its relative share to the total 16,263 vehicles included in the database, which, given a charging strategy and a vehicle type, can complete the full sequence of travels in the month without experiencing an interruption due to low battery charge. In other words, these can be considered representative of vehicle users with mobility needs that would make them good candidates to be early adopters for PHEVs/BEVs. Their share varies from 5.8% to 88.4% of the fleet, depending on the battery architecture, battery chemistry and recharge strategy. From this point forward, the analysis presented in this paper will only refer to these subsets of vehicles. Table 6 also provides the number of trips and recharges per month, as well as the trip-to-recharge ratio, a coefficient that represents how often the vehicle tends to recharge depending on the recharge constraints imposed.

Table 7 shows the average yearly mileage calculated for the scenarios from Table 6, organised into bins of 500 km/month. The bins represent different categories of users, e.g. from users that drive less than 500 km/month (6000 km/year), to users that drive more than 2000 km/month (24,000 km/year), and are key elements for classifying the EoL performance in Section 3.3. Fig. 4 depicts the overview of the mobility results, providing the distributions of the quantities reported above, i.e. monthly mileage, number of trips per month, number of recharges per month and SoC variation per recharge, for the reference vehicles and for the recharge strategies considered. The results intuitively show that vehicles with larger battery packs accumulate greater electric mileage, a higher number of electric trips, and a greater number of recharges per month. For most of the vehicles the number of trips ranges between 60 and 120 trips/month (i.e. 2-to-4 trips/day), with 15-to-30 recharging events per month in AC and 30-to-60 in DC. The higher rate of DC charging is related to the recharging constraints imposed for Strategy #2, i.e. opportunistic charging with a parking threshold of only 20 min. This scenario might be representative of a fully deployed electric fleet with widely available DC charging infrastructure. Moreover, the results show that most of the charging occurs within less than 40% of the SoC variation for PHEV-1 and BEV-1, and within less than 20% of the SoC variation for BEV-2. This is also related to the recharging constraints imposed, and other strategies such as recharging the vehicle only when the battery is below a certain threshold, would be likely to significantly change this result. Further results concerning the database from the province of Modena are reported in De Gennaro et al. (2014a,b, 2015) and Paffumi et al. (2015).

Figs. 5 and 6
depict examples of the results derived from the combinations of the battery ageing models described in section 2.3. These results are calculated with chemistry combination #4, i.e. NCM-LMO Wang et al. (2014) for calendar capacity fade and Cordoba-Arenas et al. (2015) for cycle capacity fade. Fig. 5 depicts the results by varying the recharge strategy and maintaining the same vehicle (BEV-1), whereas Fig. 6 depict the results by varying the vehicle and maintaining the same recharge strategy (recharge strategy #1).

Different usage bins are represented from left to right (if applicable to a specific scenario). Per each scenario and per each usage bin the results are projected over five time horizons: 3 months, 1 year, 3 years, 5 years and 10 years. These have been calculated by making the simplifying assumption that the activity levels recorded in the source data remain constant across time. The model results visualise the separate contributions of calendar ageing and cycle ageing to total capacity fade. White bars depict the combined capacity fade estimation assuming the reserve as per Eq. (17), (see Table 2). Per each scenario, an EoL estimation is reported in the pictures’ titles. This value is estimated by either spline interpolation or extrapolation, based on the capacity fade results calculated on the five time horizons. The results depicted in Fig. 5 show that the higher the vehicle utilization, the higher the capacity fade and the shorter the battery life. For this specific battery chemistry, calendar capacity fade is greater than cycler capacity fade, despite high vehicle utilizations. Recharge strategy does not show a major effect on the capacity fade, given the same vehicle utilization. The results depicted in Fig. 6 show the dependency of capacity fade on the vehicle type, and thus on the electric architecture of the battery. The results show a similar predicted durability trend for the vehicles PHEV-1 and BEV-1; these similarities are due to the similar electrical architecture of the battery of these vehicles, which, in both cases, are organised in 192 pouch cells cycled at C-rate and Ah-throughput equal to half of the battery values (2P-96S and 48S-2P-2S architectures). The results are significantly different for BEV-2; in fact, given the electrical architecture of their batteries, PHEV-1 and BEV-1 cycle the cells with a C-rate and Ah-throughput equal to half (50%) of the battery values while BEV-2 cycles the cells with a C-rate and Ah-throughput values equal to one seventy-second (~1.4%) of the battery value. This results in a lower cycle capacity fade according to Eqs. (1), (6), (10) and (13).

This effect is more prominent for longer time horizons, i.e. 5 and 10 years, and for high vehicle utilization bins. At the same time, this battery architecture is characterised by a high fragmentation of battery cells and complex electrical wiring, with higher costs compared to the previous two architectures. Though this architecture has the potential to increase battery lifetime, its higher upfront cost has meant it has been primarily employed in high-cost premium vehicles to date. Different results can be derived for the other battery chemistries, given the different relative weight of the cycle and calendar capacity fade mechanisms. However, the battery architecture of BEV-2 exhibits less fade over time for all the analysed chemistries.

Based on the assumptions in the previous sections, Table 8 presents EoL estimates per each recharge strategy, vehicle type and battery chemistry, resulting in 56 different scenarios. Each scenario is further broken down in the five usage bins, allowing for 220 EoL estimations in total. The results are organised in 70 boxes, each representing a specific combination of recharge strategy, vehicle and usage bin, and is representative of a certain fleet share reported below the vehicle heading. On the left side of each box are four predictions for the number of years to reach EoL from the combined application of the different calendar and cycle capacity fade models reported in Section 2.3. The right side is the predicted number of years needed to reach 100,000 km of cumulative mileage for that specific usage bin (the value is calculated using average km per month figures in Table 7). EoL is defined for all the scenarios as the point when battery capacity fade net of the reserve is below 80% of its nominal value. Each value is then coloured red if it is below 5.0 years, yellow if it between 5.0 years and 10.0 years and green if it is above 10.0 years. The colouring criterion is purely arbitrary, with the sole aim of providing the reader with a simpler visualization of the results, applying to both years to EoL and km to EoL.

The results show that users that drive up to 1000 km/month (i.e. first and second bin) experience EoL beyond 5 years except for BEV-1 with LiFePO4 chemistries and only according to model #2. Looking at the third bin, i.e. 1000–1500 km/month, the predictions highlight an increasing number of scenarios in which EoL is reached earlier than 5 years for both LiFePO4 and NCM-Mn chemistries, with significantly longer life for NCM-LMO. It is interesting to note that through the first two bins, the author’s capacity fade EoL criteria (<80% initial capacity) is normally reached before 100,000 km. Results for users in the third bin have EoL predictions that vary between the capacity fade and mileage threshold, depending on chemistry, architecture, and charging strategy. Bins 4 and 5 would be considered higher mileage users, and EoL predictions suggest that the author’s mileage threshold is more likely to be reached before the capacity fade EoL threshold for most scenarios. Several scenarios for LiFePO4 and NCM-Mn need to be intended exceptions, i.e. a PHEV is not likely to be driven fully electric for more than 100,000 km or BEV-2 is not likely to be never designed with a LiFePO4 battery. Among the scenarios considered, NCM-LMO exhibits the higher durability, with no predicted EoL below 6.2 years. BEV-1 is the only architecture that reaches the author’s capacity fade EoL threshold before the mileage threshold for recharge strategies #1 to #4. Both PHEV-1 and BEV-1 reach the capacity fade threshold before the mileage threshold for recharge strategy #5. Red scenarios are only predicted for BEV-1 and PHEV-1, while BEV-2 never shows a predicted lifetime below 10 years, due to its architecture that minimizes Ah-throughput for each individual cell and large battery capacity. It is also important to note the mutual balancing of the calendar and cycle ageing mechanisms that can be observed for the NCM-Mn and NCM-LMO chemistries of BEV-2. In fact, in almost all the recharge strategies, these chemistries do not exhibit a proportionality of the capacity fade with the usage bin, suggesting that the increase of cycle capacity fade with the mileage is mitigated by an almost equal decrease of the calendar capacity fade; this results in a vehicle that is aged independently of mileage. Additionally, by comparing recharge strategy #2 (fast charge) with the other strategies (slower charges), ageing is predicted to occur slightly more quickly at first, though this does not seem to be a dominant effect. Overall, 41 estimates out of the 220 considered for the three vehicles have a predicted life of less than 5 years (highlighted red), with more than 30% of the cases in the third, fourth and fifth bin. It is noteworthy to highlight that the scenarios related to the LiFePO4 chemistry might be affected by the deviations visible at 5% DoD and 100% DoD for 1C (see Fig. 3). The 5% DoD (i.e. 95% SoC) might be relevant, being the 100% DoD (i.e. 0% SoC) a condition never met given the model implementation assumptions in Table 2. However, this condition does not seem to frequently occur in this mobility analyses.

Table 9 reports the estimated mileage when the vehicle reaches the EoL value reported on the left side of each box of Table 8. The colour coding of the cells is the same as in Table 8. The results show that in all 41 red estimates except one (recharge strategy #5, PHEV-1, ageing model #1), the author’s capacity fade EoL threshold is reached at less than 100,000 km. Yellow and green scenarios are distributed among mileages below 100,000 km, especially in the first and second bins, and above 100,000 km, especially in the third, fourth and fifth bins. Most scenarios for PHEV-1 and BEV-1 predict the vehicles will reach the EoL threshold before the 100,000 km, while BEV-2 is characterised by higher mileages, with many scenarios predicting more than 80% capacity at beyond 300,000 km. In summary the EoL threshold is likely to be reached before 100,000 km for the red estimates, while the 100,000 km threshold is likely to be reached first for the yellow and green scenarios. Some scenarios are unlikely to be applicable for all recharge strategies, reference vehicles, and chemistries, and more in-depth analysis would be necessary before drawing specific conclusions for a single vehicle type and/or architecture.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-vehicle validation of the NCM-LMO capacity fade model against experimental data
ce:section.ce:para: This section presents the results of the experimental tests carried out by Transport Canada and Environment and Climate Change Canada for determining vehicle-level traction battery degradation. Two Nissan LEAF (BEV-1 type vehicles) were tested: one (i.e. test vehicle #1) was charged alternatively using a 50 kW DCFC charger (on-road tests) and a 7.2 kW ACL2 charger (in-lab tests), and the other (test vehicle #2) solely using a 7.2 kW ACL2 charger (Pedroso et al., 2017). The vehicles were purchased new and driven on predetermined routes in Ottawa, Canada. Vehicle CAN bus signals, e.g. battery temperature, current, voltage, and SoC, were recorded through the OBD-II port during driving and charging events. The vehicles were tested at regular intervals on a chassis dynamometer using the SAE-J1634 test procedure (SAE International, 2017). Baseline usable battery energy (UBE), corresponding to the total DC discharge energy a vehicle uses during a full depletion test on a chassis dynamometer, was measured after the run-in period and repeated at approximately 15,000, 35,000, 50,000, 62,500, and 78,000 km. The values are then compared to the baseline UBE to determine the battery degradation.

Table 10 shows a summary of the tests including: test stage ID and type, start and end calendar dates, number of recharges on ACL2 and DCFC, average recharging power in kW, driven distance in km, and driving, recharging and resting time in hours per test stage. Total mileages and time values are reported at the bottom of the table, complemented by “run-in” quantities. The “run-in” is representative of the driven distance and rest time before the test campaign was started and the vehicles instrumented for testing. Vehicle #1 was manufactured in September 2014 and vehicle #2 was manufactured in November 2014, though the test campaigns for both started in March 2015. This is important since it contributes to the calculation of the battery degradation prior to the first in-lab test.

The table shows that vehicles accumulated approximately 2000 h of driving, 1300 to 3600 h of charging and more than 30,000 h of rest time each. Test vehicle #1 was charged on DCFC for 88.9% of its mileage, and on ACL2 for the remaining 11.1%, while test vehicle #2 was always charged on ACL2, except for a single DCFC charge to troubleshoot the DCFC charger.

The recorded on-road data of test vehicles #1 and #2 was used as an input to the Wang et al. (2014) calendar fade and Cordoba-Arenas et al. (2015) cycle fade models (i.e. combination #4 for NCM-LMO in Section 2.3), along with a reserve capacity, to estimate the degradation values. These have been compared with the measured UBE. This combination of models was chosen since it represents the closest, among those available in Table 1, to the chemistry of the tested vehicles (LiMn2O4 with LiNiO2 (Inside the Battery of a Nissan Leaf, 2018; Automotive Energy Supply Corporation, 2018).

Table 11 reports the main logged quantities needed to compute the capacity fade by assuming a reserve value of 10% of the nominal battery capacity, i.e. 22 kWh usable out of 24 kWh, as per (Battery Degradation Two-Year Status Report, 2015). The Wang et al. (2014) calendar ageing model requires as input the calendar time while the vehicle is inactive, as well as the average temperature during the inactive periods. The inactive time was calculated as the calendar days between the in-lab evaluation date and the manufacturing date, minus the time spent charging and driving. Since data logging only occurred during active time, the inactive battery temperature was assumed equal to the ambient air temperature. The Cordoba-Arenas et al. (2015) cycle ageing model requires the battery temperature, battery minimum SoC, and Ah throughput during active phases. The battery temperature is assumed the average temperature the battery experiences during driving and charging events. The battery minimum SoC is calculated as the average SoC before every charging event. It is normal that the vehicles see a lower average minimum SoC during in-lab stages since the vehicles were subject to SAE J1634 full-depletion chassis dynamometer tests. The Ah throughput is calculated as the total Ah going in and out of the battery during driving and charging events. During the execution of the 52-months test campaign, there were instances when the data logger malfunctioned and did not record on-road data. The missing mileage constitutes about 3901 km of driving (on the total 185,000 km accumulated for both vehicles). The missing battery kWh and Ah were included in the calculation and estimated using driving and charging days of the same stage with similar ambient conditions. The quantities reported in Table 11 are summarised by stage to better discern the progression of the degradation as the vehicles accumulated mileage. The individual contributions of the calendar fade and cycle fade to the battery degradation model are reported, as well as the UBE degradation measured in-lab at checkpoints #x.3, #x.5, #x.7, #x.9, and #x.11 to be compared with the combined modelled results (see red bolded numbers). This comparison is also reported in the format of bar chart in Fig. 7 .

It is noticeable that the measured UBE at checkpoints #x.3 and #x.5, #x.7, #x.9, and #x.11 is computed against the checkpoint #x.1 baseline (0.0% in the table) hence not including the “run-in” phase. Instead, modelled results are referred to the nominal capacity of the battery, thus including the “run-in” phase. However, the “run-in” being relatively short compared to the accumulated mileage and resting time, the experimental and modelled values are considered comparable.

At low mileages (checkpoint #x.3), the model estimates are in good agreement with the experimental results: 3.5% model losses vs 3.6% experimental losses for test vehicle #1 and 3.4% vs. 3.4% for test vehicle #2. At checkpoint #x.5 the model forecasts 6.3% vs. the 6.7% of experimental losses for test vehicle #1 and 5.7% vs. 5.3% of experimental losses for test vehicle #2, being still in good agreement. At checkpoint #x.7 the results are still satisfying for test vehicle #1 (10.8% model vs. 10.2% experimental losses), exhibiting a significant deviation for test vehicle #2 (10.1% model vs. 7.2% experimental losses). At checkpoints #x.9 and #x.11, while the numerical results are predicting monotonically increasing losses, the experiments exhibit an inversion of the measured losses, i.e. 9.6% at #x.4 compared to 10.2% at #x.3 for test vehicle #1 and 8.6% at #x.5 compared to 9.3% at #x.4 for test vehicle #2. In the opinion of the authors, the discrepancies and the inversions measured at checkpoints #x.7, #x.9 and #x.11 can be ascribed to two major factors:
•
the higher the checkpoint, the higher the mileage, the lower the accuracy of the model. In fact, the measured cumulative Ah-throughput at cell level is approximately 7000 Ah at checkpoint #x.3 and 16,000 Ah at checkpoint #x.5, to increase to 22,000, 29,000 and 36,000 Ah at checkpoints #x.7, #x.9 and #x.11. The models presented in section 2.2 rely on experimental datasets of cells aged with Ah-throughput values in the order of 10,000 (more in some cases, however below 20,000). It is therefore likely that the models can accurately predict the losses until applied within the Ah-throughput values from which they were derived, failing the forecasts at higher values, i.e. 20,000–30,000 Ah or more;
•
it is likely that the measured results at higher mileage are affected by a behaviour of the BMS that is not taken into accounts in the models. In fact, it is likely that the more the battery is aged, the more the BMS allows to use the “reserve” of the battery capacity, to avoid the user to experience a range drop. This can be affected by an overall evaluation of several parameters affecting the SoH of the battery, e.g. SoC, Ah-throughput, temperature, etc., providing the BMS with a set of values used the basis for making the best decision for each specific vehicle. Clearly this is not represented in the models and goes beyond the scope of the present work.

Despite these assumptions, the presented work constitutes a step beyond the state-of-the-art in the topic of in-vehicle battery durability and a unique case study on its own. It is among the first scientific work that combines calendar and cycle capacity fade models with large-scale real-world driving data.

This paper addresses a scenario-based analysis of in-vehicle performance degradation of automotive traction batteries in real-world use conditions developing 220 scenarios by combining three Li-ion variants in three different battery architectures with five different recharging strategies, and breaking down the vehicle usage in five bins, based on the monthly mileage. Different calendar and cycle capacity fade performance-based models available in the literature are scaled-up to battery-level and combined with real-world driving patterns for calculating average years and kilometres to EoL, defined as the battery capacity drops below 80% of its nominal value. The results show that most of the combinations of Li-ion chemistries, battery architectures, and recharge strategies do not lead to battery capacity drop below 80% of its nominal value (EoL) in less than 5 calendar years for a driving profile of up to 1000 km/month. At higher monthly mileage, the differences among the durability performance of the different chemistries become more evident, with NCM-LMO exhibiting the higher durability, with no predicted EoL below 6.2 years. Concerning the architectures, the BEV-2 (16S-72P-6S) allows for the maximum durability; this is because it allows for minimising the Ah-throughput for each individual cell, mitigating the relative weight of the cycle effects in the overall capacity fade. The work also includes validation of the model at vehicle-level by using real-world measurements from two test-vehicles from a test campaign carried out by Canadian Government Departments (Transport Canada and Environment and Climate Change Canada). It shows that the modelled capacity fade values are in good agreement with the experiments at low mileage (i.e. checkpoints #x.3 and #x.5), exhibiting increasing deviation compared to experiments as mileage, and therefore Ah-throughput, increases.

The presented approach aims, for the first time, at combining big data from transport with capacity fade models for traction batteries accounting for different electrical architectures, Li-ion chemistries, recharge strategies and usage patterns, identifying which elements are most likely to determine the End of Life (EoL) of the battery. This approach can either provide the scientific community with a comprehensive overview of real-world scenarios on the subject or serve as basis for determining the key influencing parameters and the needed assumptions to extend the analysis to different regions of the world or to future battery chemistries and architectures. The work has been presented within the 24th and 25th sessions of the Electric Vehicle and Environment Informal Working Group (EVE-IWG) (UN/ECE, 2018), with the aim of supporting the decision making process for in-vehicle battery durability within the UN’s World Forum for Harmonization of Vehicle Regulations (WP.29).
The views expressed here are purely those of the authors and cannot, under any circumstances, be regarded as an official position of the Institutions or Departments with which they are affiliated.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to express their gratitude to Michael Safoutin and Michael Olechiw from the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA, US), Kendelle Anstey from Environment and Climate Change Canada, Martha Christenson from Transport Canada for the support given in shaping the contents of this paper. The authors wish also to thank Dragan Simic, for the support in editing the Figs. 5 and 6.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Abraham et al., 2005
